content:
5 min read
Just now

--

When I started building MediFind landing page for my university project which is a cross-platform pharmacy locator and ordering platform for Sri Lanka authentication was the last thing on my mind. To be honest our supervisor suggest this thing and then actually that clicks me. And I was deep in Flutter animations, Google Maps integrations, and making the 3d models for this website. And this option is at the very end of the page and this security thing felt like something I would “add later.”
That changed when I launched the MediFind web landing page and added a pharmacy partner onboarding flow. Pharmacies needed to register, submit their business details, and be verified before going live on the platform. Suddenly, I needed real authentication. not just a form that collected emails. I told it cause we were panicked and gather all the ideas from our team and two members suggest that shall we link a google form when pharmacy clicks the ‘Get Started’ button’. So that’s not a good suggestion and I was search everywhere how to do it means instantly, hustle free and to be honest with making an entire authentication system.
That’s when I found WSO2 Identity Platform (formerly Asgardeo).
What Is WSO2 Identity Platform?
WSO2 Identity Platform is a CIAM which means cloud-native Customer Identity and Access Management solution. It just allows developers add secure authentication, user registration, and their authorization to any application if it’s a web, mobile, or API without building any of that infrastructure yourself. Which is absolute life saver when it comes to a situation like we were in.
Think of it as the identity layer for your app. Instead of managing plenty of passwords, tokens, and user stores yourself, you delegate all of that to WSO2’s battle-tested platform, and your users get a smooth, seamless, secure sign-in experience.
And when I searching about it I found out that t supports industry-standard protocols that including OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect (OIDC), and SAML . Literally which means it works with any tech stack.
The Problem I Was Solving
As I told briefly in previously my MediFind web platform has a section where pharmacies can apply to join as partners. Previously, the “Get Started” button did nothing it was just a UI placeholder. Actually It was something that need me to do but because I was the team leader and has plenty of thing to do with the app and and web I thought I’m gonna do it later.
So the real flow I needed was:

• When A Pharmacy clicks “Get Started” button

• They are securely authenticated (sign up or sign in)

• After authentication, they fill in their pharmacy details such as their name, their pharmacy registration number (BR number), city and contact number

• They submit their application
Without proper authentication, anyone could submit fake applications. right. and that’s exactly what our supervisor also question from us. to be honest. So I needed a way to verify that the person submitting the form is a real, verified user with a confirmed email address.
Then the WSO2 Identity Platform solved this headache perfectly within like couple of minutes.
How I Integrated It
I used the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow with PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) which is the recommended approach for single-page applications because it doesn’t require storing a client secret in the browser.
Here’s how the integration works at a high level:
Step 1 — Create an application in WSO2 Identity Platform
I signed up for a free account at asgardeo.io, created an organization called “medifind” with simple ‘m’ cause remember it doesn’t allow capital leters, and registered my web app as a Single Page Application. I configured the authorized redirect URL to point to my GitHub Pages deployment.
Step 2 -Then implement PKCE in the browser
Cause our MediFind Web is a made by usinf complete plain HTML/JavaScript site (no framework), I implemented the PKCE flow directly using the Web Crypto API. Here I just add that snippet:
async function generatePKCE() {
const verifier = base64url(crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(32)));
const hashed = await crypto.subtle.digest('SHA-256',
new TextEncoder().encode(verifier));
const challenge = base64url(hashed);
return { verifier, challenge };
}So the verifier is stored in sessionStorage, and the challenge is sent to WSO2’s authorization endpoint. When the user completes login and is redirected back, we exchange the authorization code plus the verifier for an access token and ID token.
Step 3 - Redirect to WSO2 login
When the pharmacy clicks “Get Started”, they are redirected to WSO2’s hosted login page……like this:
const params = new URLSearchParams({
response_type: 'code',
client_id: CLIENT_ID,
redirect_uri: REDIRECT_URI,
scope: 'openid profile email',
state,
code_challenge: challenge,
code_challenge_method: 'S256',
});
window.location.href = `${ASGARDEO_BASE}/oauth2/authorize?${params}`;WSO2 handles all the login UI, email verification, and password management. New pharmacies can register directly from the WSO2 login page. it was perfect means it even allows me to upload my own product logo and product color to their platform.
Step 4 -Handle the callback and show the registration form
After a successful login, WSO2 redirects the user from their platform back to MediFind with an authorization code. We exchange this for tokens, decode the ID token to get the user’s email, and display the pharmacy registration form pre-filled with their verified email.
const payload = JSON.parse(atob(data.id_token.split('.')[1]));
showRegistrationForm(payload);What the User Experience Looks Like
From the pharmacy’s perspective, the flow is smooth and seamless:

• They land on the MediFind website and click “Get Started”

• They are taken to a clean WSO2-branded login page with all our MediFind’s logo and colors

• They sign up with their email or sign in if they already have an account

• Their email is automatically verified by WSO2

• They are redirected back to our MediFind where a pharmacy registration form appears, pre-filled with their verified email

• They complete the form and submit their application
No fake registrations. No password management headaches. No email verification code to build. WSO2 handles all of it. literally everything.
Why I’d Choose WSO2 Identity Platform Again
It’s free to get started. The free tier is generous enough for a startup or student project like ours. There’s no credit card required to set up and test the full authentication flow.
It’s production-grade. WSO2 is used by enterprises worldwide. Integrating it into a student project immediately elevates the technical credibility of the work.
PKCE is the right approach. Many tutorials still show the implicit flow for SPAs, which is deprecated. WSO2’s documentation correctly steers developers toward PKCE, and implementing it taught me how OAuth 2.0 actually works under the hood. Not just how to import a library and call signIn().
It handles the hard stuff. Email verification, password reset, account recovery, brute-force protection …..all built in. As a developer, I can focus on the product, not the identity infrastructure.
What I Learned
Integrating WSO2 Identity Platform taught me a lot about how authentication actually works at the protocol level. Before this, I had used libraries like NextAuth or Firebase Auth and mostly treated auth as a black box. Implementing PKCE manually - generating code verifiers, hashing challenges with SHA-256, exchanging authorization codes for tokens, decoding JWT payloads. just gave me a genuine understanding of the OAuth 2.0 flow.
It also made me appreciate why standards matter. Because WSO2 uses OpenID Connect, the same integration approach would work with any OIDC-compliant provider. The knowledge transfers.
If you’re building any web application that needs user authentication especially one where you’re onboarding a specific type of user like a business partner or verified professional WSO2 Identity Platform is absolutely worth exploring.

So and need to mention that MediFind is a cross-platform pharmacy locator and ordering platform currently in development for the Sri Lankan healthcare market. The web platform is live at achinthajayaweera.github.io/Medifind-web.
